title: Discipleship, Part 6: The Wife as the Husband's Discipler
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Discipleship 6 - The Wife as the Husband’s Discipler - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: 1. Review: The Manner of the Wife's Submission
subpoints: Willing submission: the verb is middle voice -- 'submit yourselves'; not coerced but voluntary
Christian submission (Colossians 3:18 -- 'as is fitting in the Lord'): a non-submissive Christian wife is a contradiction in terms
Specific submission: to her own husband, not to men in general
Respectful submission (Ephesians 5:33): she does not gossip about or disparage her husband

outline.title: 2. Exceptions to the General Rule of Submission
subpoints: The husband cannot ask the wife to worship false gods or worship the true God falsely
Cannot ask her to participate in sexual immorality, lying, murder, or physical abuse
Physical abuse falls under the murder commandment -- she is not called to silently endure it
Caution: do not expand exceptions to cover everything we don't want to do; if it takes more than 12 steps to connect something to a sin, examine your heart

outline.title: 3. The Dignity of Women: Submission Does Not Imply Inferiority
subpoints: The most profound example of submission is Jesus himself -- he submitted to the Father but was never inferior to the Father
Colossians 3:11: in Christ there is neither male nor female in terms of standing and worth
1 Peter 3:7: husband and wife are co-heirs of the grace of life -- no essential difference before God
Genesis 3:16: the curse makes submission unnatural; only a regenerated heart can practice what Paul describes

outline.title: 4. The Wife as Discipler of Her Husband Through Submission
subpoints: By submitting to her husband as the church submits to Christ, she teaches him what his own submission to Christ looks like
1 Peter 3:1-2: a wife's conduct can win even an unbelieving husband without a word; how much more a believing husband
1 Peter 3:3-6: the wife disciples her husband through her godliness -- a gentle and quiet spirit, a peaceful demeanor
Proverbs 21:19; 26:21 -- a quarrelsome, nagging spirit is corrosive; a peaceful spirit draws her husband toward Christ

outline.title: 5. The Wife as Helper: Genesis 2:18-20
subpoints: The word 'helper' (ezer) outside of Genesis 2 is used almost exclusively of God helping man -- a divine quality of help
Nothing else in all creation could be that helper to man; only the woman was suited to fulfill this calling
She provides the kind of help that furthers the man's calling and life mission
Being the man's helper is her means of discipling him -- pointing him to Christ, keeping him accountable, ministering to him

summary:
This sixth installment of the Discipleship series continues the examination of marriage as a venue for discipleship, focusing on how the wife disciples her husband. Pastor Tito Lyro begins by reviewing the four characteristics of the wife's submission established in Part 5: it is willing (middle-voice verb -- 'submit yourselves'), Christian (as is fitting in the Lord, Colossians 3:18), specific (to her own husband, not to men in general), and respectful (she does not disparage her husband).

The lesson then addresses exceptions to the general rule of submission: the wife is not called to submit if the husband asks her to sin -- whether worship of false gods, sexual immorality, lying, murder, or physical abuse. The last example is given particular weight: physical abuse falls under the murder commandment, and she is not called to endure it silently. The lesson cautions against expanding exceptions too broadly; if it takes many mental steps to connect something to a sin, the heart should be examined.

A substantial section affirms the dignity of women and argues that submission does not imply inferiority. The example of Jesus submitting to the Father -- without any ontological inferiority -- provides the theological framework. Colossians 3:11 and 1 Peter 3:7 establish that in Christ there is no difference between men and women in standing and worth. Genesis 3:16 explains why submission feels unnatural: it is the result of the curse, and only a regenerated heart empowered by the Spirit can practice the kind of submission Paul describes.

The lesson then turns to the main thesis: the wife disciplines her husband through her submission, her godliness, and her role as his helper. From 1 Peter 3:1-6, the wife's chaste and godly conduct can draw even an unbelieving husband toward Christ without a word. The 'gentle and quiet spirit' of 1 Peter 3:4 is not emotionlessness but a peaceful, non-contentious demeanor. Finally, Genesis 2:18 introduces the wife as 'helper' (ezer) -- a word used almost exclusively elsewhere in the Old Testament for God helping man. She is divinely suited to help her husband in a way no other creature can, and in that helping she disciples him.
transcript:
## Discipleship, Part 6: The Wife as the Husband's Discipler

*Pastor Tito Lyro -- Sunday School*

---

We've been studying discipleship in the adult class. We saw that discipleship is the constant process in which a Christian is helped by the covenant community to grow in the grace and knowledge of Jesus Christ, so that he becomes progressively conformed to the image of Jesus Christ, and can in turn disciple others. Discipleship is done in the context of the local church -- the Great Commission was given to the church; baptism is given to the visible church.

A couple of weeks ago, we learned that the discipleship also happens in families -- in marriage. The husband and the wife are discipling each other. We started by looking at the role of the wife, and in order to understand marriage as a relationship in which discipleship takes place, we need to understand the overall roles of the husband and wife. I want to start by reviewing what we covered last time, and then move forward.

Ephesians 5:22-24:

> *Wives, submit to your own husbands, as to the Lord. For the husband is head of the wife, as also Christ is the head of the church; and he is the Savior of the body. Therefore, just as the church is subject to Christ, so let the wives be to their own husbands in everything.*

The command to submit means the wife will defer to her husband in everything -- that's the general rule. And implied in the wife's command to submit is the command for the husband to lead.

### The Manner of the Wife's Submission

**First, it is a willing submission.** The New King James doesn't show it, but in the original language the verb is middle voice -- "submit yourselves" -- a reflexive verb. This is something the wife does willingly, not something she is forced to do. Douglas Moo comments that submission "suggests a voluntary willingness to recognize and put oneself under the leadership of another." Your submission to the Lord is willing; your submission to your husband should be the same.

**Second, it is a Christian submission.** Colossians 3:18 says: *"Wives, submit to your own husbands, as is fitting in the Lord."* A submissive wife is consistent with -- fitting with -- who Christ is and what he did for her. In the Bible, "non-submissive Christian wife" is a contradiction in terms; they are two different buckets that cannot coexist. John Calvin says: *"Wives cannot obey Christ without yielding obedience to their husbands."* The expression "as fitting in the Lord" also means the husband is not the ultimate head of the wife -- Christ is. So the husband has no right to tell the wife to believe or act contrary to what Christ says.

**Third, it is a specific submission.** Ephesians 5:22 says *"your own husbands"* -- a wife is to be submissive to her own husband, not to every man. She does not owe any special submission to other men just because she is a woman. She submits to civil authorities and church authorities just like every man does. But this particular kind of submission is to her own husband.

**Fourth, it is a respectful submission.** Ephesians 5:33: *"Let the wife see that she respects her husband."* She does not gossip about him, does not portray him in a dark light unnecessarily. She respects him because of his position and because that is what she is called to do.

### Exceptions to the General Rule

Now, there are things the husband cannot ask the wife to do -- things where submitting to him would be failing to submit to Christ:

- The husband cannot ask the wife to worship false gods.
- He cannot ask her to worship the true God falsely.
- He cannot ask her to participate in sexual immorality.
- He cannot ask her to lie.
- He cannot ask her to be involved in murder or physical abuse. This falls under the umbrella of the murder commandment: she is not called to silently endure being beaten. Call the police, call the elders, get help.
- He cannot ask her to commit robbery -- and the Larger Catechism helps us think broadly about what that includes.

Having said that, I plead with you: do not try to fit everything you don't want to do under one of these categories. If it takes more than twelve steps of mental lawyering to connect something to a sin, look at your heart. Theologians speak of "straight-line" issues vs. "jagged-line" issues. If what the husband is asking is a straight-line sin, you know it. If you are having to do a lot of mental gymnastics, examine your motives.

### The Dignity of Women: Submission Does Not Imply Inferiority

Now I want to talk about the dignity of women -- because we must teach both things together.

The most profound example of submission is the Lord Jesus himself. Jesus came into the world in submission to the Father, but at no time was he inferior to the Father. It was a *functional* submission, not an *ontological* one. The Son is not something less than the Father in his essence. It is different roles that the Trinity agreed to assume in order to accomplish God's will.

So husbands: if you believe your wife should submit to you because you are superior to her, repent. That is a lie from the pit of hell. You are not superior to your wife.

Colossians 3:11 says: *"In Christ there is neither Greek nor Jew, circumcised nor uncircumcised, barbarian, Scythian, slave nor free, but Christ is all and in all."* There is no essential difference between regenerated men and women -- they are all sinners in need of salvation; they are all regenerated the same way; they have the same glory in the sight of God.

1 Peter 3:7: *"Husbands, likewise, dwell with them with understanding, giving honor to the wife, as being heirs together of the grace of life."* Co-heirs. No essential difference before God.

So marriage is a partnership in which we are called to different *roles*. Man is not better than woman by virtue of being man. Woman is not better than man by virtue of being woman.

**Why doesn't submission come naturally?** Genesis 3:16: *"To the woman he said: '...Your desire shall be for your husband, and he shall rule over you.'"* This "desire" is not romantic longing -- it means her desire will be *for the role of her husband*. She will not be satisfied with God's design for her. She will want to take over his position. That is the natural tendency of the sinful heart -- and it is true of the husband as well, who will tend toward either authoritarianism or abdication.

Only a regenerated heart, in which the Spirit of God dwells, is able to practice the kind of submission Paul describes. All the grace you need to obey this command is already yours at the cross of Jesus Christ.

### The Wife as Discipler of Her Husband

Now we can consider how this looks like as discipleship from the wife's perspective.

**Through submission itself:** Ephesians 5:24 -- as the wife submits to her husband, she shows him how he himself must submit to Christ. In the analogy Paul uses, the husband plays the role of the church in relation to Christ. By practicing biblical submission, the wife is teaching her husband what Christian submission to Christ looks like.

**Through godliness:** 1 Peter 3:1-2:

> *"Wives, likewise, be submissive to your own husbands, that even if some do not obey the word, they, without a word, may be won by the conduct of their wives, when they observe your chaste conduct accompanied by fear."*

A wife's godly conduct can soften even an unbeliever's heart and draw him toward Christ. How much more does this apply to the believing husband who has the Spirit of God working in him? The wife's piety, her walk with God, will have a profound impact on her husband. Be a godly woman -- that is how you help your husband follow Christ more closely.

1 Peter 3:3-6:

> *"Do not let your adornment be merely outward... rather let it be the hidden person of the heart, with the incorruptible beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit, which is very precious in the sight of God."*

What matters is the work of the Lord in her heart and a peaceful demeanor. The gentle and quiet spirit is not emotionlessness -- the Bible is not saying be a subdued, no-personality person. It is saying: do not be a quarrelsome, contentious person. Proverbs 21:19: *"Better to dwell in the wilderness than with a contentious and angry woman."* Proverbs 26:21: *"As coals are to burning coals and wood to fire, so is a contentious man to kindle strife."* A peaceful spirit draws her husband toward Christ.

**Through being his helper:** Genesis 2:18-20:

> *"And the LORD God said, 'It is not good that man should be alone; I will make him a helper comparable to him.'"*

God brought all of creation before Adam, and nothing was found to be that helper. Then God created the woman. The word *helper* (ezer) is significant: outside of this passage, this word is used almost exclusively in the Old Testament to describe *God helping man* -- "God is our help" (Psalm 121). So the woman has this unique, divinely-given capability to help the man in a way that no other man can do, no other creature can do.

She is the divine helper to her husband. And as she fulfills that role, she is the means through which God helps her husband: pointing him to Christ, keeping him accountable, ministering to him in concrete ways. In being the helper, she disciples her husband.

Let's pray.
